title: Dynamic Equilibrium with Costly Short-Selling and Lending Market
abstract: We develop a dynamic model of costly stock short-selling and lending market and obtain implications that simultaneously support many empirical regularities related to short-selling. In our model, investors‚Äô belief disagreement leads to shorting demand, whereby short-sellers pay shorting fees to borrow stocks from lenders. Our main novel results are as follows. Short interest is positively related to shorting fee and predicts stock returns negatively. Higher short-selling risk can be associated with lower stock returns and less short-selling activity. Stock volatility is increased under costly short-selling. An application to GameStop episode yields implications consistent with observed patterns.

article.1 Economy with Costly Short-Selling:
JEL G11 - Portfolio Choice; Investment Decisions G32 - Financing Policy; Financial Risk and Risk Management; Capital and Ownership Structure; Value of Firms; Goodwill Editor: Stefano Giglio Stefano Giglio Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

A significant fraction of stock trades in markets nowadays emerges from the activities in the stock short-selling and lending market.1The key imperfection in this market is that to borrow a stock, a short-seller must pay a shorting fee to the stock lender. Corresponding to the growth in the stock short-selling and lending market, a vast empirical literature (elaborated on below) has developed investigating the effects of costly short-selling on the shorting fee, stock price, stock risk premium and volatility, short interest and its predictive power, short-selling risk (uncertainty about future shorting fees) and short-selling activity (volume). The existing theoretical studies (discussed below), however, are not suitable to address many of the empirical regularities documented in this literature. This is because, static models cannot meaningfully shed light on the findings of stock volatility, the predictive power of short interest, and the short-selling risk, all of which require a dynamic model at the minimum. On the other hand, the shorting fee and the short interest are typically specified exogenously or are deterministic in the extant dynamic models, which limit their ability to explain some of these empirical regularities.

In this paper, we fill this void and provide a comprehensive analysis of the costly stock short-selling and lending market within a familiar dynamic asset pricing framework. Our model delivers a simple equilibrium generating rich implications and offers straightforward intuitions for all our findings. Taken together, our paper makes several contributions. First, to our best knowledge, ours is the first theory work to simultaneously reconcile the extensive empirical evidence on the behavior of the shorting fee, stock price, its risk premium and volatility, short interest and its predictive power, and short-selling risk and activity. Second, our model delivers novel implications on the much documented predictive power of short interest for future stock returns, as well as on the short-selling risk, stock volatility, effects of noise traders and lenders‚Äô size in the stock lending market, which are not in the prior theoretical literature. Third, we shed light on the recent GameStop episode. Finally, our methodological contribution and the tractability of our model is due in large part to our ability to identify all investors‚Äô subjective risk premiums when they face different market imperfections (Section 1.4).

Specifically, we develop a tractable dynamic equilibrium model that incorporates costly stock short-selling and lending following the usual market practices. Investors‚Äô time-varying belief disagreement on the stock payoff generates demand for short-selling and supply of lendable shares. The (pessimistic) short-sellers borrow stock shares from the (optimistic) lenders by paying ashorting fee, which along with the stock price and short interest, are determined endogenously in equilibrium. Thus, in our model, the shorting fee is determined by the classic supply-and-demand channel, for which there is much evidence (e.g.,Nagel 2005;Blocher, Reed, and Van Wesep 2013;Prado, Saffi, and Sturgess 2016). In the ensuing equilibrium, the shorting fee and short interest arise as time-varying processes with stationary steady-state distributions, enabling our model to shed light on some of the regularities that the extant works cannot. We first identify investors‚Äô subjective stock risk premiums, which are different across investors because in addition to the usual capital gains/losses and dividends for nonlending investors, holding the stock long yields additional income for lenders, whereas having a short position in the stock generates additional costs for short-sellers.

We then determine the equilibrium and find that the costly-to-short stock shorting fee is risky since it is driven by a time-varying disagreement process so that a higher belief disagreement leads to a higher shorting fee, consistent with the empirical findings ofD‚ÄôAvolio (2002). This result occurs because not all optimistic investors are lenders and those who are lenders can only lend a part of their stock holdings, a feature we refer to aspartial lending, which may be interpreted as a binding lending constraint.2Hence, a higher disagreement leads to a lesser increase in lending supply than the increase in shorting demand, and so for the stock lending market to clear the equilibrium shorting fee increases. We next show that the stock price is positively related, while its risk premium negatively related to its shorting fee, a result well-supported by empirical evidence (e.g.,Jones and Lamont 2002;Ofek, Richardson, and Whitelaw 2004;Cohen, Diether, and Malloy 2007;Blocher, Reed, and Van Wesep 2013;Prado 2015;Drechsler and Drechsler 2016;Duong et¬†al. 2017). This result arises because the stock is ultimately held by optimistic investors, be it lenders or nonlenders. As the disagreement, and thus the shorting fee, increases these optimistic investors increase their stock demand and reflect their views more (especially lenders due to the additional stock lending income). This higher demand leads to a higher current stock price and lower subsequent returns, consistent with the classicMiller (1977)argument.

We further find that the costly-to-short stock volatility is greater in our economy than that in an economy with costless short-selling, even though the stock has the same time-varying disagreement in both economies. This result is supported by the empirical evidence inSaffi and Sigurdsson (2010)andDrechsler and Drechsler (2016). In our model, it arises because the fluctuations in the shorting fee due to disagreement shocks lead to more volatile stock price changes. We also investigate how the key economic quantities are affected by the lenders‚Äô size in our economy. We find that the equilibrium shorting fee and stock price decrease, while the stock risk premium increases in the lenders‚Äô size, as supported by empirical evidence (Nagel 2005;Prado, Saffi, and Sturgess 2016). These relations arise because as more investors become lenders, the stock lending supply increases, leading to a lower shorting fee, and thus to a lower stock price and higher subsequent returns on average.

We next turn to the stockshort interest, a widely used measure to infer the amount of short-selling for a stock. We first determine the short interest and show that it is positively related to shorting fee, consistent with the empirical evidence (D‚ÄôAvolio 2002;Beneish, Lee, and Nichols 2015;Drechsler and Drechsler 2016).This result arises because, a higher current shorting fee corresponds to a higher current disagreement and a higher stock price. Now that the short-sellers are relatively more pessimistic, they increase their shorting demand, leading to the positive relation between the short interest and the shorting fee.

We then obtain our key implication that the costly-to-short stock current short interest predicts its future returns negatively. This predictability arises in our model because, as discussed above, a higher short interest corresponds to a higher current shorting fee and disagreement. The higher lending income and more optimism induce lenders to increase their stock demand leading to an inflated current stock price, which in turn experiences lower shocks in subsequent periods on average leading to lower future returns. This finding is strongly supported by the vast evidence both at the individual stock level and at the aggregate market level (Seneca 1967;Figlewski 1981;Senchack and Starks 1993;Desai et¬†al. 2002;Asquith, Pathak, and Ritter 2005;Boehmer, Huszar, and Jordan 2010;Beneish, Lee, and Nichols 2015;Rapach, Ringgenberg, and Zhou 2016). Among themBeneish, Lee, and Nichols (2015)show that this predictability effect is stronger for costly-to-short stocks, which lends further support to our underlying mechanism, since we also find that the predictive ability of short interest is solely due to the presence of shorting fees (i.e., a costless-to-short stock short interest can still be time-varying but does not predict future returns). Therefore, our model reveals that the current short interest is an ‚Äúinformative‚ÄĚ signal for future returns for costly-to-short stocks, but not for stocks that are costless-to-short.3

We next shed light on the recent empirical findings ofEngelberg, Reed, Ringgenberg (2018), who show that stocks with highershort-selling risk(higher uncertainty about future shorting fees) have lower returns and lessshort-selling activity(volume). In our model, because of the time variation in disagreement, short-sellers‚Äô demand and lenders‚Äô stock supply also fluctuate, which in turn lead to a time variation in the shorting fee, resulting in short-selling risk. We find that short-selling risk matters in equilibrium, and show that a higher short-selling risk can be associated with lower stock returns and less short-selling activity as inEngelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018)due to the differences in stock partial lending. The negative relation between the short-selling risk and risk premium arises because the shorting fee of a stock with a low degree of partial lending is more sensitive to the disagreement shocks. This is because such a stock has a lower lending supply, which can only absorb the short-selling demand by increasing the shorting fee, leading to a higher shorting fee variance, as well as a higher stock price and a lower risk premium. Similarly, the negative relation between the short-selling risk and short-selling activity occurs because a lower partial lending also leads to a lower short-selling activity since short-sellers now short less for any given disagreement level. In sum, our theory suggests that for two similar costly-to-short stocks, the one lent to short-sellers in greater proportion by lenders will have less short-selling risk, higher future returns, and more short-selling activity.

We also generalize our model to additionally feature noise traders with exogenous random stock demands and possible presence in the stock lending market. This more general setting enables us to shed light on the recent GameStop episode. This analysis is motivated by events in early 2021 that due to a large new retail investor demand, GameStop and other ‚Äúmeme‚ÄĚ stocks experienced sharp increases in stock prices and shorting fees, as well as decreases in short interests. We first find that the costly-to-short stock shorting fee and price increase and its short interest decline significantly in response to a large noise trader demand, consistently with the regularities during the Gamestop episode. These results arise because even though the noise traders are only directly present in the stock market and not in the short-selling market, their stock demand still creates distortions in the short-selling and lending market. As more of the stock supply is held by noise traders, the stock lending supply decreases, leading to a higher shorting fee. The higher shorting fee then leads to a further increase in the stock price, over and above the direct increase from the positive noise trader demand. As for the short-sellers, they reduce their positions because first, they anticipate the large noise trader demand to persist. Second, the noise trader distortions in the short-selling and lending market amount to a lower stock lending supply and higher shorting fees (discussed above), inducing short-sellers to reduce their short position further. Given that above effects maybe undesirable, we propose a novel economic mechanism to alleviate these effects based on increasing the stock lending supply by noise traders. Indeed, we show that inducing noise traders to lend some of their stock holdings would lead to a lower shorting fee and stock price and a higher short interest.

Our paper is related to the large theoretical literature studying the effects of short-sale constraints and restrictions in the stock market. The works in this literature typically examine the effects of investors not being able to hold short (i.e., negative) stock positions, which in static settings includeMiller (1977),Diamond and Verrecchia (1987), andHong and Stein (2003), and in dynamic settings includeHarrison and Kreps (1978),Detemple and Murthy (1997),Scheinkman and Xiong (2003),Gallmeyer and Hollifield (2008),Chabakauri (2015), andSimsek (2021).

More closely related papers in this literature are those in which investors can hold short positions by paying a stock shorting fee, as in our model.4However, these works are typically fairly stylized and primarily focus on the effects of the shorting fee on the stock price and risk premium only. Naturally, some of our results on these quantities are also present in some of these works. In particular, under static settingsBlocher, Reed, and Van Wesep (2013)andBanerjee and Graveline (2014), and under dynamic settingsDuffie, G√Ęrleanu, and Pedersen (2002),Daniel, Klos, and Rottke (2021), andG√Ęrleanu, Panageas, and Zheng (2021)find that a higher stock shorting fee leads to a higher stock price and lower future expected returns, as in our paper. Moreover,Duffie, G√Ęrleanu, and Pedersen (2002),Blocher, Reed, and Van Wesep (2013), andDaniel, Klos, and Rottke (2021)also agree with our result that the shorting fee increases in disagreement. Our paper, however, differs from these works in terms of its methodology and our key results. Specifically,Duffie, G√Ęrleanu, and Pedersen (2002)feature a search and bargaining-based model with risk-neutral investors in which the stock is a claim to a single payoff, whileDaniel, Klos, and Rottke (2021)feature a model with a series of one-period optimization and exogenous short interest, andG√Ęrleanu, Panageas, and Zheng (2021)consider an overlapping-generations model to show short selling can lead to ‚Äúrun-type‚ÄĚ behavior and multiple equilibrium. In contrast, our main economy is fairly standard with risk averse investors, with the stock being a claim to a dividend flow rather than a single payoff, featuring no search and bargaining considerations, and is cast in a fully dynamic economy in which the equilibrium is unique and short interest is determined endogenously. More importantly, because of these modeling differences, none of the above works has our results on the stock volatility, predictive power of the short interest over a long horizon, short-selling risk and activity, the effects of noise traders and their participation in the stock lending market.5

Finally, this paper is also related to the vast literature on heterogeneous beliefs in financial markets (Detemple and Murthy 1994;Zapatero 1998;Basak 2000,2005;Johnson 2004;David 2008;Yan 2008;Dumas, Kurshev, and Uppal 2009;Cvitanińá and Malamud 2011;Banerjee 2011;Simsek 2013;Bhamra and Uppal 2014;Ehling, Graniero, and Heyerdahl-Larsen 2017;Atmaz and Basak 2018;Andrei, Carlin, and Hasler 2019). However, none of these works considers the costly stock short-selling and lending market as we do, hence do not have our main mechanisms and results.
In this section, we develop a tractable model of costly stock short-selling and lending market within a familiar dynamic asset pricing framework.6Investors‚Äô belief disagreement leads to the presence of stock lenders and short-sellers in the economy. For clarity, we consider a single-stock economy in which the stock represents a typical costly-to-short stock. However, our framework can easily accommodate a multiple-stock extension, capturing the cross-sectional differences in short-selling costs in reality for which all our results remain valid (Remark 5 of Section 3;Appendix C).

Remark 1 (Further discussion of investor types). Neither the equal mass nor the belief symmetry among investor types is necessary for our main mechanism and results to hold, and they can be generalized in a straightforward manner. We consider this specification because it allows us to isolate the effects of costly short-selling by providing a clear benchmark economy in which without short-selling costs, the belief disagreement alone does not affect the ensuing equilibrium stock prices (as highlighted in Section 2). This way our model is also in line with the empirical evidence showing that, consistent with the classic Miller (1977) argument, one needs both belief disagreement and short-selling costs to have a systematic effect on stock prices (e.g., Boehme, Danielsen, and Sorescu 2006 ).

Remark 2 (Further discussion of disagreement processes). In Appendix A , we discuss the microfoundations of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck type Gaussian disagreement process in (2) by considering a Bayesian learning environment, in which investors are symmetrically informed but have different interpretations of signals as often considered in the dynamic differences in beliefs models with stationary disagreement (e.g., Scheinkman and Xiong 2003 ; Dumas, Kurshev, and Uppal 2009 ; Xiong and Yan 2010 ). We also note that our disagreement measure őł t is also the standard deviation of investors‚Äô expectations on the stock dividend, a commonly employed measure of belief disagreement.

Moreover, it is well-known that in a framework like ours, that is, constant absolute risk aversion (CARA) preferences with Gaussian payoffs, the stock prices can admit negative values with positive probabilities. Due to being a Gaussian process, now additionally the disagreementőłtin(2), as well as the ensuing equilibrium shorting fee that turns out to be driven by the disagreement (Proposition 1), can take on negative values with positive probabilities. However, our model allows us to control these probabilities and obtain almost always positive shorting fee and disagreement for suitable choices of parameter values. 1.3 Stock short-selling and lending market

In reality, short-sellers sell stocks that they do not own with the anticipation of making a profit from future stock price declines. To do so, they (effectively) pay a shorting fee to borrow shares from lenders who are long in the stock, and sell those borrowed shares to other willing buyers, who are not necessarily lenders of the stock.8Accordingly, we incorporate costly short-selling and stock lending market into our economy by capturing the standard market practices briefly discussed above. Toward that, and also in anticipation of the equilibrium outcomes of Sections 2‚Äď4, we further categorize the optimists and pessimists populations into two groups with respect to their ability to participate in the stock short-selling and lending market.

Thelenders,‚Ąď‚Ā†, with a population massőĽ, are the optimistic investors who lend a fractionőĪ0<őĪ‚Č§1of their long position in the stock to short-sellers. We refer toőĪaspartial lendingthat also can be thought of as utilization, the fraction of lending supply actually lent out in the data. As we elaborate on in Remark 3 below, the partial lending feature also may be interpreted as a binding lending constraint. The remaining optimistic investors areholders,h, with a population massőĽ0.5‚ąíőĽ‚Ā†, and they do not participate in the stock short-selling and lending market. AsD‚ÄôAvolio (2002)discusses, we need such investors to help clear the stock market as ‚Äúthe outstanding securities must come to rest with nonlending investors willing to hold these securities despite forgoing the loan fees capitalized into the equilibrium price.‚ÄĚ

Theshort-sellers,s, with a population massőĽs, are the pessimistic investors who can borrow from lenders by paying for each stock share theshorting feeŌēŌēt‚Ā†, which is to be determined endogenously in equilibrium. Finally, the remaining pessimistic investors arenonparticipantswith a population massőĽ0.5‚ąíőĽs‚Ā†, and they do not participate in the stock short-selling and lending market. By being pessimistic and not participating in the short-selling market, in equilibrium, nonparticipants do not hold any stock positions, and therefore their presence is not crucial in the determination of the endogenous stock price or the shorting fee, and hence for our main mechanism and results. Therefore, without loss of generality, we set their population mass to 0 (i.e.,őĽőĽs=0.5‚Ā†), and consider an economy with three types of investors‚Ąď‚Ā†,h, ands.9

Remark 3 (Further discussion on partial lending and holders). Our partial lending assumption of optimistic lenders loaning only a fraction őĪ of their long position to short-sellers helps us obtain a realistic shorting fee in a simple way and also enables us to make a distinction between short interest (fraction of outstanding shares held by short-sellers) and lending supply (fraction of outstanding shares available for lending), as in the data. Moreover, in our model the partial lending parameter őĪ straightforwardly maps into its data counterpart, utilization (the fraction of lending supply actually lent out to short-sellers) and empirical evidence suggests utilization is far less than unity. For instance, Saffi and Sigurdsson (2010) , Aggarwal, Saffi, and Sturgess (2015) , and Beneish, Lee, and Nichols (2015) show that a typical stock has an average utilization around 20%. Aggarwal, Saffi, and Sturgess (2015) further find that stocks on special (i.e., stocks with shorting fees higher than 1%) have an average utilization around 43%, a value we set for őĪ in our numerical illustrations as discussed in Appendix B .

The partial lending feature in our model also may be interpreted as a binding lending constraint. To illustrate this interpretation, inAppendix D, we consider a simple version of our framework in which optimistic lenders choose a fractionxtof their long stock position to lend subject to a lending constraintőĪxt‚Č§őĪ‚Ā†, whereőĪis some constant controlling the tightness of the lending constraint. As we show inAppendix D, in such a framework, a positive shorting fee arises only when the lending constraint bindsőĪ(xt=őĪ)‚Ā†, since lenders lend out as many shares as possible if shorting fee is positive. The lending constraint interpretation also may be justified by realism since according to Section 18(f) of the Investment Company Act, U.S.‚Äďregulated funds cannot lend more than one-third of assets under management at any point in time.10Given that mutual funds are the main lenders in the short-selling market, this regulatory restriction prevents most lenders from loaning all their shares.

Relatedly,h-type investors (holders) in our model are completely restricted from lending their long stock positions. Their presence in our model ensures the existence of an equilibrium even in the full lending economy and also may be justified on several realism grounds. For instance, because of regulatory restrictions, some funds, such as unit investment trusts (UITs) in the United States, are not allowed to lend. Moreover, even though they are allowed to lend, most active mutual funds do not lend the stocks they hold long. For instance,Honkanen (2021)finds that at the end of 2018, only 28% of U.S. active funds (as opposed to 61% of passive index funds) lent some of their stock holdings. Similar findings for German mutual funds is reported byGreppmair et¬†al. (2020), who find only 25% of active funds (as opposed to 74% of passive index funds) lent stocks. Reputational concerns also may be preventing some investors such as firm founders and management executives, from lending their long positions in their firms so as to not be seen as helping short-sellers. Finally, retail investors typically do not lend their shares, andBlocher and Zhang (2022)argues that this nonlending behavior may be due to the belief that withholding shares from short sellers could enhance their expected returns.

The third term, if exists, adjusts for the additional shorting fee cost or lending income from being a short-seller or a lender in equilibrium, respectively. If an investor turns out to be a lender in equilibrium, she must be an optimist and earning a shorting feeŌēŌētfor each stock share she lends out, which is a fractionőĪof her long stock position, thereby increasing her subjective stock risk premium by an amountőĪŌēőĪŌēt‚Ā†. Similarly, if an investor turns out to be a short-seller in equilibrium, she must be a pessimist and paying a shorting feeŌēŌētfor each stock share she borrows, thereby increasing her (negative) subjective stock risk premium by an amountŌēŌēt‚Ā†.11The subjective and objective risk premiums will be determined endogenously in equilibrium.

Our explicit identification of the investor type-specific risk premiums above facilitates much tractability in our subsequent general equilibrium analysis in which investors face different market imperfections. This in turn allows us to employ familiar stochastic dynamic programming techniques in the determination of equilibrium.
article.2 Shorting Fee, Stock Price and Its Dyna:
In this section, we solve for the equilibrium in which investors face different market imperfections, and recover the effects of costly short-selling on the stock. In particular, we find that the equilibrium shorting fee is time-varying and increases in disagreement, the stock price is positively related, while the risk premium is negatively related to the shorting fee, and the stock volatility is increased, all consistently with empirical evidence.

The costly short-selling and lending market economy is said to be inequilibriumif the stock priceS, the shorting feeŌēŌēand the investors‚Äô consumption and portfolio strategiesŌą(ci,Ōąi)‚Ā†,i=‚Ąď,h,s‚Ā†, are such that (a) all investors choose their optimal consumption and portfolio strategies given the stock price, the shorting fee, and their beliefs, (b) the stock market and the short-selling and lending market clear, that is,őĽŌąőĽŌąŌąőĽŌąt‚Ąď+(12‚ąíőĽ)Ōąth+12Ōąts=Q‚Ā†, andőĽőĪŌąŌąőĽőĪŌąt‚Ąď+12Ōąts‚Č•0‚Ā†, at all timest, respectively.

We will often make comparisons with the costless short-selling benchmark economy represented as an upper bar(¬Į)‚Ā†. In this frictionless benchmark economy, only the stock market needs to clear in equilibrium (as in standard asset pricing models) and pessimistic investors can short-sell without incurring any costs (i.e.,ŌēŌēt=0‚Ā†). With costly short-selling, the stock short-selling and lending market needs to satisfy an additional clearing condition. In particular, the equilibrium shorting fee for the stock must be such that the total amount of its shares shorted is no greater than the total amount lent. In fact, we let the short-selling and lending market clearing condition to hold with equality, which leads to a unique shorting fee, whose average value can be mapped to the observed high shorting fee of a typical expensive-to-short stock in the data (e.g., 696 bps inDrechsler and Drechsler 2016).12Thus, in our model, the shorting fee is determined by the classic supply-and-demand channel, for which there is much evidence (e.g.,Nagel 2005;Blocher, Reed, and Van Wesep 2013;Prado, Saffi, and Sturgess 2016) as we will discuss later when presenting our results for Proposition 1.13

In this costly short-selling economy, there are two sources of uncertainty and one risky security available for trading for all investors, and hence markets are dynamically incomplete. We employ the standard stochastic dynamic programming method (Merton 1971) to solve for each investor‚Äôs optimal consumption and portfolio strategies and apply market clearing conditions to obtain the equilibrium. Proposition 1 reveals that the equilibrium shorting fee(7)is risky since it is driven by disagreement and the fluctuations in the disagreement lead to time variation in the shorting fee so that a higher belief disagreement leads to a higher shorting fee (property a).14This result arises because not all optimistic investors are lenders and those who are lenders can only lend a part of their stock holdings, and so a higher disagreement leads to a lesser increase in lending supply than the increase in shorting demand. Hence, for the stock lending market to clear the equilibrium shorting fee must increase. This result is consistent with the empirical evidence inD‚ÄôAvolio (2002), who shows that the shorting fee of a stock is high when the investor disagreement is high.

Turning to the equilibrium stock price and its dynamics, we first see that its price in the benchmark economy with costless short-selling is as in the standard economy with a single investor with unbiased beliefs. With costly short-selling, the equilibrium stock price(8)and its risk premium(9)have simple structures and are additionally affected by the shorting feeŌēŌē‚Ā†. The implication here is that the stock price is positively related, while its risk premium is negatively related to the shorting fee (property b) consistently with vast empirical evidence (e.g.,Jones and Lamont 2002;Ofek, Richardson, and Whitelaw 2004;Cohen, Diether, and Malloy 2007;Blocher, Reed, and Van Wesep 2013;Prado 2015;Drechsler and Drechsler 2016;Duong et¬†al. 2017).15In our model this result arises because the stock is ultimately held by optimistic investors. As the disagreement, and hence the shorting fee, increases these optimistic investors increase their stock demand and reflect their views more (especially lenders due to the additional stock lending income). This higher demand leads to a higher current stock price and lower subsequent returns.16We also note that the stock price reflecting the views of the optimistic investors more under higher short-selling costs is consistent with the classicMiller (1977)argument illustrated in a static setting, thus demonstrating that such a classic argument survives in a dynamic setting.

We also find that the stock volatility is higher than that in the benchmark economy with costless short-selling (property c) even though the stock has the same time-varying disagreementőłtin both economies. In the absence of short-selling costs (and wealth transfer effects), the belief disagreement itself does not affect stock prices. In contrast, under costly short-selling, the fluctuations in the shorting fee due to disagreement shocks lead to more volatile stock price changes (Figure¬†1, panel D).17This result is consistent with the empirical evidence inDrechsler and Drechsler (2016), who find that expensive-to-short stocks have higher stock return volatility compared to almost-costless-to-short stocks. Similarly,Saffi and Sigurdsson (2010)find a positive relation between high shorting fees and high volatility. On the other hand, investors‚Äô value function(11)has a familiar structure that typically arises in dynamic linear equilibrium asset pricing models with Gaussian processes.

We also look at the effect of lenders‚Äô sizeőĽon the equilibrium quantities inFigure¬†1. Panels A‚ÄďC illustrate that the equilibrium shorting fee and stock price decrease, while the stock risk premium increases in the lenders‚Äô size, respectively. These relations arise because as more investors become lenders, the stock lending supply increases, leading to a lower shorting fee, and thus to a lower stock price and higher subsequent returns on average. The empirical support for these effects are provided byPrado, Saffi, and Sturgess (2016), who show that stock ownership composition matters for the stock lending market and stock prices. In particular, they find that stocks with more concentrated institutional ownership, that is, stocks that are held by fewer institutions but with larger stock holdings, corresponding to lower lenders‚Äô sizeőĽin our model, have lower lending supply, higher shorting fees, and lower future returns on average. Likewise,Nagel (2005)finds that shorting fee is negatively associated, while future stock returns are positively associated with institutional ownership, a plausible proxy for the lenders‚Äô size in our model.18

As discussed in the Introduction, a number of existing theoretical works find results similar to ours that a shorting fee increases in disagreement and a higher shorting fee is positively associated with a higher stock price and a lower risk premium. Our findings in Proposition 1, properties a and b complement these works. However, our findings on volatility (Proposition 1, property c), the effects of lenders‚Äô size, which are illustrated inFigure¬†1, and all our findings in the subsequent Sections 3 and 4 (Propositions 2‚Äď4) are all novel.

Remark 4 (Discussion on economy with search frictions). In Appendix E , we consider an alternative static economy in which short-sellers must pay a search cost to locate long investors so as to borrow shares and establish a short position. In this economy, an endogenous positive shorting fee arises even in the absence of lending constraints or partial lending. We show that our results in Proposition 1 and Figure¬†1 that the shorting fee is increasing in disagreement, the stock price is increasing in shorting fee, and both the shorting fee and the stock price are decreasing in lenders‚Äô size continue to hold under search frictions with exogenous search intensity.

In the economy with search frictions, the equilibrium search intensity is endogenously determined by equating the short-sellers‚Äô marginal cost of searching to the increase in their expected utility of being able to locate a long investor and trade. We show that when short-sellers can endogenously choose their search intensity, an increase in disagreement may actually decrease the shorting fee. This result arises because as disagreement increases, pessimistic investors become more pessimistic, which increases the benefit of meeting a long investor and trade, resulting in more aggressive search efforts in equilibrium. By increasing their search intensity, short-sellers now meet with more optimistic investors, leading to an increase in lenders size, which results in a lower shorting fee, similar to the effect of lenders‚Äô size in our main model. If the search cost function is sufficiently convex, this endogenous response of search intensity is relatively inactive, and the equilibrium behaves very much like an exogenous search intensity model, which in turn behaves like the present partial lending model. In this sense, a search-based model with sufficiently convex search costs provides another microfoundation for our partial lending feature.
article.3 Short Interest and Short-Selling Risk:
In this section, we examine the behavior of the short interest and short-selling risk (uncertainty about future shorting fee) of the costly-to-short stock. We find that the equilibrium short interest is increasing in the shorting fee and predicts future stock returns negatively, supporting the vast empirical evidence. We further find that short-selling risk matters in equilibrium, and show that a higher short-selling risk can be associated with lower stock returns and less short-selling activity, also consistently with the recent evidence.

3.1 Short interest

A widely used and closely watched measure to infer the amount of short-selling for a stock is its short interest , which is the fraction of its outstanding shares held by short-sellers. Vast empirical evidence documents that a higher current short interest predicts lower future stock returns, both for individual stocks and for the aggregate stock market itself. Proposition 2 reveals that the short interest is positively related to shorting fee (property a), which is also illustrated inFigure¬†2.19This is because, a higher current shorting fee corresponds to a higher current disagreement and a higher stock price, and hence the short-sellers are relatively more pessimistic now and increase their shorting demand, leading to the positive relation between the short interest and the shorting fee of the stock. The key implication of Proposition 2 is that the short interest predicts future stock returns negatively, implying a higher current short interest tends to be followed by lower stock prices (property b). This predictability arises in our model because, as discussed above, a higher short interest corresponds to a higher current shorting fee and disagreement. Hence, lenders increase their stock demand due to a higher lending income and more optimism, leading to an inflated current stock price, which experiences lower shocks on average in subsequent periods leading to lower future returns. Here, we would like to highlight that the predictive ability of short interest is solely due to the presence of shorting fee. This is because in the benchmark economy, the short interest is still time-varying (as it is driven by disagreement), but it does not predict future returns since stock price does not depend on disagreement when the short-selling is costless. Therefore, our model shows that the current short interest is an ‚Äúinformative‚ÄĚ signal for future returns for costly-to-short stocks, but not for stocks that are costless-to-short.

In the literature, empirical investigations of whether or not the short interest predicts future returns date back toSeneca (1967), who finds that high short interest predicts lower future returns for the S&P 500. Subsequent research also confirmed these findings for both at the individual stock levels and at the aggregate market level. For the individual stock levels, for example,Desai et¬†al. (2002)show that stocks with a high short interest experience lower returns and this predictability persists up to 12 months. Similarly,Boehmer, Huszar, and Jordan (2010)show that stocks with a low short interest in the previous month experience higher returns in the next month. Similar findings are also inFiglewski (1981),Senchack and Starks (1993),Asquith, Pathak, and Ritter (2005), andBeneish, Lee, and Nichols (2015). Among themBeneish, Lee, and Nichols (2015)show that the effect is stronger for stocks with higher shorting fees, which is in line with our finding that in the benchmark economy short interest is still time-varying but does not predict future returns. For the aggregate market,Rapach, Ringgenberg, and Zhou (2016)find that a higher aggregate short interest predicts lower future stock market returns, and in fact argue that the short interest is the strongest known predictor of aggregate stock market returns. On the theory side, however, to our knowledge, our analysis is the first to demonstrate that a higher short interest predicts lower future stock returns for various horizons, as well as obtaining an endogenous short interest with a stationary steady-state distribution.

Moreover, several empirical studies additionally show that, in addition to the short interest (a stock variable), a positive change in short interest (a flow variable) also predicts lower future returns (Boehmer, Jones, and Zhang 2008;Diether, Lee, and Werner 2009). Our model is also consistent with these findings since the slope coefficient in the regression of future stock returns on past changes in short interestŌĄőĪő≤ŌĄŌĶŌĄSt+ŌĄ‚ąíSt=őĪőĒSI+ő≤őĒSI(SIt‚ąíSIt‚ąíŌĄ)+ŌĶt+ŌĄ,is negative. Furthermore,Diether, Lee, and Werner (2009)also show that the current short selling is positively related to past stock performance. Our model is also consistent with this finding since the slope coefficient in the regression of the current short interest on past stock price changesőĪő≤ŌĄŌĶSIt=őĪőĒS+ő≤őĒS(St‚ąíSt‚ąíŌĄ)+ŌĶt,is positive.20We also highlight that since the predictive ability of the short interest is due to the presence of the shorting fee, our results here also imply that the shorting fee is an important predictor of future stock returns. This is well supported by the recent empirical work ofEngelberg et¬†al. (2020), who show that the shorting fee is the single best predictor of future stock returns in the cross-section.

3.2 Short-selling risk

In sum, our model offers a possible explanation for the evidence inEngelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018)by demonstrating that the differences in partial lending across stocks may very well be behind their findings.24In particular, our theory suggests that for two similar costly-to-short stocks, the one lent to short-sellers in greater proportion by lenders will have less short-selling risk, higher future returns on average, and more short-selling activity.

In recent related work,Muravyev, Pearson, and Pollet (2022)look at the predictive power of short-selling risk.25They find that short-selling risk predicts unadjusted stock returns in univariate regressions, consistent with the findings ofEngelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018). However, they also show that short-selling risk does not predict (a) unadjusted returns in multivariate regressions when other control variables, such as short interest and utilization, are included, and (b) stock returns adjusted for the shorting fee (net-of-fee returns) in univariate regressions. Since in our model the negative relation between the stock risk premium and short-selling risk is due to the differences in partial lending, which also corresponds to utilization (i.e., fraction of lending supply actually lent out, see also Remark 3), our theory would also suggest that this negative relation would disappear once controlled for utilization, in line with the first predictability finding ofMuravyev, Pearson, and Pollet (2022). Moreover, looking at the relation between the short-selling risk and stock risk premium adjusted for shorting fee (net-of-fee) in our model, we find that this relation ceases to be negative and in fact it can be slightly positive for our baseline calibration. That being said, we also highlight that our results in this section are based on the relation between the instantaneous stock risk premium and short-selling riskŌēVŌē‚Ā†, which is constant in our model as expression(15)reveals. To be able to investigate the predictive ability of short-selling risk for future returns meaningfully, one would ideally consider a predictive regression similar to the one in(12)for the short interest. Of course, for this one would need a model that generates a time-varying short-selling risk, an issue we leave for future research.

Remark 5 (Multiple-stock economy). Our model and results have been presented in the context of a single-stock economy so as to highlight our insights for the costly-to-short stock as clearly as possible. That being said, even though the short-selling costs can be fairly significant for many stocks, for plenty others these costs can be quite low and they can be shorted fairly cheaply in today‚Äôs markets. For instance, Drechsler and Drechsler (2016) find the average shorting fee for a typical stock in the most expensive-to-short and cheapest-to-short deciles to be 696 basis points and 2 basis points, respectively. To provide robustness that all our results for the costly-to-short stock remain valid in the presence of other stocks, we consider a two-stock generalization of our economy in Appendix C in which the first stock represents a typical costly-to-short stock, while the second stock represents a typical cheap-to-short stock, whose shorting fee, for clarity, is constructed to be zero in equilibrium.

We find that for the costly-to-short stock, the equilibrium shorting fee is increasing in disagreement, the stock price is positively related, while the risk premium is negatively related to the shorting fee, and the stock volatility is higher than that in the benchmark economy, as in our main model (Proposition 1). We further show that the equilibrium short interest predicts future stock returns negatively and higher short-selling risk can be associated with lower stock returns and less short-selling activity, as also in our main model (Propositions 2 and 3;Figure¬†3). The same results arise in this extended two-stock economy because the introduction of an additional costless-to-short stock does not alter the main underlying economic mechanisms at play for the costly-to-short stock as discussed in Sections 2 and 3. For instance, a higher disagreement in costly-to-short stock still leads to a lesser increase in lending supply than the increase in shorting demand, leading to an increase in shorting fee.
article.4 Application to the GameStop Episode:
In this section, we generalize our model to a richer setting to additionally feature noise traders with exogenous random stock demands and possible presence in the stock lending market. This setting enables us to shed light on the recent GameStop episode where, due to a large new retail investor demand, some stocks referred to as ‚Äúmeme stocks‚ÄĚ experienced sharp increases in stock prices and shorting fees, while experiencing sharp reductions in short-selling.26We again obtain analytical solutions for all quantities of interest and demonstrate that all of our results remain valid in this richer economy. Moreover, our model reconciles the observed patterns around the GameStop episode by showing that a large positive noise trader stock demand leads to much higher stock prices and shorting fees, while also leading to lower short interests. We offer a novel economic mechanism based on increases in stock lending supply to alleviate these effects, which are often argued to be detrimental to the efficient workings of financial market.27

We incorporate noise traders into our model by taking their population mass to be m and scaling the mass of existing investors‚Äô, that is, lenders, holders, and short-sellers, by 1 ‚ąí m ‚Ā† . Figure¬†4reveals that the equilibrium shorting fee increases with more noise traders present (panel A).33This result shows that even though the noise traders only hold stocks without participating in the short-selling and lending markets, their stock demand creates distortions in these markets. As more of the stock supply is held by noise traders who do not lend their stock positions, the stock lending supply decreases, which leads to higher shorting fees, consistent with empirical findings ofLi (2022)that long-only institutions reduced their positions.Figure¬†4also shows that the equilibrium stock price increases in the noise traders‚Äô size (panel B). This arises from two channels. The first is the obvious one that a large direct stock demand of noise traders pushes the stock price up. The second channel is due to higher shorting fees (panel A), thereby leading to a further increase in the stock price (Proposition 1, property b).

We also see that the equilibrium short interest decreases in the noise traders‚Äô size (panel C). This result appears somewhat surprising given our finding in panel B, since it states that rather than increasing their positions as the stock price increases, short-sellers actually reduce their positions. This behavior occurs in our model because, first, (risk-averse) short-sellers anticipate the large direct stock demand of noise traders (and hence the stock price appreciation) to persist over time, so they reduce their short position. Second, as discussed above, the presence of noise traders causes distortions in the stock short-selling and lending market. More noise traders in the economy corresponds to a lower stock lending supply and higher shorting fees, which for a given disagreement level, again induce short-sellers to reduce their positions.34This result underscores the fact that even in the absence of wealth effects and other market frictions (e.g., margin and funding frictions), our model mechanism has the elements of a ‚Äúshort squeeze‚ÄĚ due to short-sellers being risk averse.35As highlighted earlier, our findings above support the regularities during the GameStop episode. That said, we also note that one can obtain similar results in our model without noise traders by considering a reduction in partial lendingőĪőĪ.Indeed, a lower partial lending can increase the shorting fee and the stock price, while simultaneously decreasing the short interest. This equivalence arises because an increase in noise traders means an average long-holder lends a lower fraction of her shares, effectively a reduction inőĪ. However, given the evidence of retail investor activity during the GameStop episode, we believe the mechanism that we highlight that depends on the presence of retail investors may more realistically describe and map into the GameStop episode.

We further note that our short interest result here is not inconsistent with our earlier short interest result in Proposition 2 property a. In our earlier analysis without noise traders, the positive relation between the short interest and shorting fee arises because both quantities increase in disagreementőłt, thus, resulting in perfect positive correlation between these two endogenous quantities. Our analysis here shows that, for a given noise trader demand, a higher disagreementőłtraises the short-selling demand, increasing both the shorting fee and short interest. On the other hand, for a given disagreement level, a higher noise trader demandZt, reduces the supply of lendable shares, increasing the shorting fee but reducing the short interest. To better understand the overall relation between the short interest and shorting fee in equilibrium, in panel D ofFigure¬†4, we plot the correlation between them.36We see that the correlation is still positive unless the noise trader size/demand is too large. For instance, for our baseline calibration, the short-selling demand (disagreement) channel is dominant generating a positive correlation unless the total new noise trader demand as a fraction of stock supply is at least around 50%, an unrealistically high value.

From the analysis above, it is clear that the effects of noise traders on the equilibrium stock price and quantities in the stock short-selling and lending market can be undesirable. It would be of interest to see whether these adverse effects can be alleviated within our model. Here, we argue for one economic mechanism based on inducing noise traders to lend some of their stock holdings for such an alleviation. Figure¬†5reveals that the equilibrium shorting fee and stock price decrease, while the short interest increases in the noise traders‚Äô lending fractionőĪZ. These results arise because when noise traders are induced to lend more of their long stock holdings, the stock lending supply increases, leading to a lower shorting fee, which in turn leads to more short selling and a lower stock price.37Thus, our results here demonstrate that having the noise traders lend some of their stock holdings can have stabilizing effects in the stock and short-selling markets.38That being said, we also acknowledge that there may be other mechanisms that can lead to similar effects in these markets. For example, the retail-oriented brokerages imposed trading and margin restrictions in the midst of the GameStop episode and asJones, Reed, and Waller (2021)show these restrictions also led to stock price decreases.
article.5 Conclusion:
In this paper, we provide a comprehensive analysis of the costly short-selling and lending market within a familiar dynamic asset pricing framework. Our model generates rich implications that simultaneously support the extensive empirical evidence on the behavior of a costly-to-short stock shorting fee, price, its risk premium and volatility, short interest and its predictive power, and short-selling risk and activity, and also offer simple straightforward intuitions for them.

Our main novel results are as follows. We show that the equilibrium short interest is positively related to the shorting fee and predicts future stock returns negatively. We also find that higher short-selling risk can be associated with lower stock returns and less short-selling activity. We additionally show that the stock volatility is increased in the presence of costly short-selling. Finally, applying our model to the GameStop episode, we find the shorting fee and stock price to increase and the short interest to reduce significantly in response to a new large stock demand.

So as to not unnecessarily complicate our model, we do not consider some specific features of the actual stock short-selling and lending practices. For instance, in our model 100% of the short-selling proceeds are kept as collateral (see proof of Lemma 1 inAppendix A). This rate is very close to that in the US for domestic stocks, for which lenders typically require 102% of the short-selling proceeds as a collateral to help protect themselves (D‚ÄôAvolio 2002). Moreover, in our model, lenders get all of the shorting fee upon lending a share. In reality, this is true for large institutions with internal lending facilities, which directly lend to short-sellers. Other lenders typically use an agent bank/brokerage and receive only a fraction of the shorting fee. Our framework would be able to accommodate these generalizations. Relatedly, our framework may also accommodate other regulatory interventions, such as the short-selling bans and their welfare consequences as inBuss et¬†al. (2016), benchmarking incentives of the lending institutions as inBasak and Pavlova (2013), additional trading fees as inBuss and Dumas (2019). We leave these considerations for future research.

Appendix C: Economy with Multiple Stocks

Given that there is a significant heterogeneity in short-selling costs in the cross-section, in this Appendix, we provide robustness of our results for the costly-to-short stock in the presence of other stocks. Towards that, we consider a simple extension of our framework by introducing an additional, costless-to-short, stock. We demonstrate that all our main results and underlying economic mechanisms remain valid in this more elaborate economy.

The multistock economy we consider here is the simplest and the most straightforward extension of our single-stock setting of Section 1, also admitting much tractability. Proposition C1 reveals that the costly-to-short stock equilibrium shorting fee and price as well as all the other equilibrium quantities have structures similar to the corresponding ones in the single-stock case. Consequently, the underlying economic mechanisms discussed in Sections 2 and 3 for the costly-to-short stock are also present in this setting with two stocks. This is simply because the introduction of an additional (costless-to-short) stock does not alter the optimistic lenders and pessimistic short-sellers‚Äô behavior for the costly-to-short stock in the lending market. For instance, a higher disagreement in costly-to-short stock still leads to a lesser increase in lending supply than the increase in shorting demand, leading to an increase in shorting fee.

Since the underlying economic mechanisms and the structures of the economic quantities in this two-stock setting are as in our main model, all the implications for a costly-to-short stock are also as before, as highlighted in the properties a‚Äďe of Proposition C1. We also find that the costly-to-short stock‚Äôs short-selling risk can still be associated with lower stock returns and less short-selling activity, as demonstrated inFigure¬†3of our main model. For the zero-fee stock 2, we see that, unsurprisingly, its equilibrium quantities are as in the benchmark economy with costless short-selling. In sum, our analysis in this section demonstrates that the introduction of an additional stock does not alter our results for the costly-to-short stock as discussed in Sections 2 and 3 but it can help map the model to data better given the heterogeneity in short-selling costs across stocks in reality.

Proposition E1 reveals that an endogenous positive shorting fee arises in the economy with search frictions even in the absence of lending constraints. Moreover, as(E.4)shows, the positive shorting fee arises when the belief disagreement is sufficiently large, in line with our earlier findings. Properties a and b also highlight that our main economy results in Proposition 1 that the shorting fee is increasing in disagreement and the stock price is increasing in shorting fee continue to hold with search frictions for a given search intensityőĽ. We also find that both the shorting fee and the stock price are decreasing in the search intensity. This result arises because, by increasing their search intensity, short-sellers now meet with more optimistic investors, leading to an increase in lenders size, which results in a lower shorting fee and stock price, similar to the effects of lenders‚Äô size in our main model (Figure¬†1).

The equilibrium search intensityőĽis determined by short-sellers‚Äô first-order condition(E.6), which equates the marginal cost of searchingőĽc‚Ä≤(őĽ)to the increase in short-sellers‚Äô expected utility from being able to locate a long investor and trade. When short-sellers can endogenously choose their search intensityőĽ, then an increase in disagreementőłmay decrease the shorting fee. In this case, we obtain the optimal search intensity in closed-form in(E.13), which shows that the search intensity is positively related to disagreement, a feature we also illustrate inFigure E1, panel A. This positive relation is intuitive since as disagreement increases, pessimistic investors become more pessimistic, which increases the benefit of meeting a long investor and trade, resulting in more aggressive search efforts in equilibrium.Figure E1, panel B, illustrates that through the endogenized search intensity, an increase in disagreement can now lead to a lower shorting fee, an effect opposite of the case when search intensity is exogenous. This result arises because, as discussed above, a higher search intensity leads to a higher lenders‚Äô size and a lower shorting fee. That said, the empirical evidence of a positive relation between investor disagreement and shorting fees (e.g.,D‚ÄôAvolio 2002) suggests that the endogenized search intensity channel may not be quantitatively large in reality.

In sum, both our main model and this model with search frictions share the common results that the shorting fee is increasing in disagreement and the stock price is increasing in shorting fee. Moreover, since search intensity is also the size of lenders in this model, the result that both the shorting fee and the stock price are decreasing in the search intensity is also consistent with our main model finding for the lenders‚Äô size. However, this model with search frictions produces an opposite prediction on the effects of disagreement through the endogenous search intensity that cannot be obtained in our main model. That said, our main model also have several predictions that cannot be obtained in this model with search frictions, such as the results on the stock volatility, predictive power of the short interest over a long horizon, short-selling risk and activity.

7 The exogenously specified interest rate is suitable for our purposes for several reasons. First, most studies on short-selling costs are cross-sectional in nature (e.g., studying return differences for cheap-to-short vs. expensive-to-short stocks) for which the common interest rate does not play a role. Second, economically it is difficult to imagine short selling costs of expensive-to-short stocks to have any material effect on aggregate quantities, particularly on the market interest rate, which is determined in a relatively very large market where foreign institutions also play a significant role. In fact, to the best of our knowledge there is no empirical evidence on interest rates being affected by the short-selling fees of individual stocks. So, by taking the interest rate as exogenous, we avoid any potential spurious effects that may arise due to modeling choices.

8 The exact mechanics of stock short-selling are somewhat more involved, but our above description captures their essentials (for an extensive discussion of short-selling, see D‚ÄôAvolio 2002 ; Reed 2013 ). This interest income is shared between the short-seller and the lender. The short-seller‚Äôs account earns the rebate amount, while the lender‚Äôs account earns the shorting fee, which is effectively what short-sellers pay to lenders for each share they short. Moreover, Kolasinski, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2013) and Chague et¬†al. (2017) provide evidence of dispersion in shorting fees. In our model there is one type of lender, leading to a unique shorting fee, which can be viewed as the average shorting fee.

14 The equilibrium shorting fee (and other economic quantities) are also affected by the parameters of the disagreement process, lenders‚Äô size őĽ , partial lending őĪ , as well as the risk adjustment term ő≥ ŌÉ ő≥ ŌÉ 2 Q ‚Ā† , through the endogenous constants a , b , A , and B .

15 We note that even though we focus on the effects of the shorting fee on the risk premium in Proposition 1, we find that the stock mean return őľ t and the Sharpe ratio ŌÄ ŌÉ ŌÄ t / ŌÉ are also negatively related to the shorting fee.

16 We note that in our model, for a very high shorting fee, stock risk premium can be negative and also can be lower than that in the benchmark economy even after adjusting for shorting fees (net-of-fee), in line with the evidence in Drechsler and Drechsler (2016) .

17 With constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) preferences, it is well-known that, due to the wealth transfer effects, the belief disagreement itself can generate more volatile stock prices even in the absence of short-selling costs (e.g., Dumas, Kurshev, and Uppal 2009 ; Atmaz and Basak 2018 ). Therefore, with such preferences, one could plausibly obtain an implication that costly short-selling leads to a less volatile stock price since it restricts trading, and thus the wealth transfer among investors. That being said, as we discuss above, the empirical evidence shows that expensive-to-short stocks typically have high stock return volatility, which suggests that for those stocks short-selling costs may have a more dominant effect than the wealth transfer channel.

18 As Figure¬†1 , panel D, illustrates, the stock volatility does not vary much with lenders‚Äô size őĽ , thereby implying that two stocks with the same level of high shorting fee but differing in lenders‚Äô size, and hence differing in lending supply, may have similar volatility. In our model this occurs because the fluctuations in the disagreement is now mostly absorbed by the fluctuations in shorting fee, whose marginal effects on the stock price is smaller compared to fundamental dividend shocks. This finding may help us understand the somewhat surprising evidence in Saffi and Sigurdsson (2010) and Kaplan, Moskowitz, and Sensoy (2013) , who do not find a significant relation between the lending supply and stock volatility.

20 We omit the analysis of these two results for brevity, but they can be shown in a straightforward manner following the steps of the proof of Proposition 2.

21 Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) find that even though the shorting fee of a typical stock is low on average, it experiences large fluctuations over time, creating significant short-selling risk for investors. For example, they find that for a typical stock, the 1 s t percentile of its shorting fee is 7 bps on average, while the 99 t h percentile is 301 bps, indicating a significant variation in shorting fees over time. Moreover, they find the mean (median) number of days for a short position to be open to be 81 (65) days, indicating a significant exposure to the short-selling risk for investors. Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) further show that stocks with higher short-selling risk have less price efficiency, where their price efficiency measure is computed by regressing the weekly stock returns on the current and the lagged (value-weighted) market returns, which is something we cannot capture meaningfully in our framework.

22 As is well recognized in the case of trading volume, employing the standard definition of short-selling volume, by taking the absolute value of the short interest changes, | d S I t | ‚Ā† , in a continuous-time setting is problematic since the local variation of the driving uncertainty (Brownian motion) is unbounded. The measure ŌÉ ŌÉ S I does not suffer from this issue and captures the unexpected short-selling volume by not taking into account of the expected changes in the short interest.

23 We also find a similar result if instead of short interest volatility (a flow measure) we use the short-interest itself (a stock measure) for the short-selling activity. We demonstrate our results using the short interest volatility to be consistent with Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) , who also employ a flow measure.

26 At the beginning of January 2021, GameStop shares traded at a price of around $18, with a shorting fee of around 20% and a short interest of 140%. Then, because of a sudden large retail trader demand, GameStop stock rallied by 1,400% in January. In particular, in the last week of January 2021 alone GameStop stock price soared 400% during which its shorting fee increased to 38% for existing shorts (the shorting fee for the new short were reported to be over 100%), while the number of GameStop shares that were shorted decreased more than 50% during that week (see, e.g., https://www.reuters.com/article/us-gamestop-stocks-shortsellers/number-of-gamestop-shares-shorted-falls-by-over-half-in-week-s3-partners-idUSKBN2A12WE ). GameStop carried on experiencing large fluctuations in these quantities for many months.

27 During the GameStop episode, policy makers and regulators were greatly concerned about these adverse effects on financial markets, and called for investigations and more regulations (see, e.g., https://www.wsj.com/articles/gamestop-stock-reddit-and-robinhood-what-you-need-to-know-11611960243 ).

28 Akin to our specification for disagreement (2) , the noise trader demand is a time-varying Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process as (17) illustrates. The special case of őļ őļ Z = Z 0 = 0 corresponds to the noise trader demand being zero on average, that is, ‚Äúwhite noise.‚ÄĚ We consider the more general case since it enables us to apply our model to the recent GameStop episode, where there was a large positive stock demand from new retail investors for a sustained period of time. Moreover, for the GameStop case specifically, one could also model the noise trader demand Z to be present for some time only, say during the period [ T 1 , T 2 ] ‚Ā† , which could itself be modeled as a random period governed by the arrival times of a Poisson process. In this case, during the period prior to T 1 , the equilibrium quantities would be as in our main model of Section 1 if existing investors did not anticipate the sudden noise trader demand, which was perhaps the most likely case in the GameStop episode. However, if the existing investors were to anticipate the possibility of a future period [ T 1 , T 2 ] during which there was a large noise trader demand, then the equilibrium quantities also would be additionally affected by the expected arrival of this period, as well as the expected noise trader demand during the period prior to T 1 . Our framework would be able to accommodate these more complex generalizations, though we believe our simpler setting sufficiently captures the essentials of the noise trader demand.

31 We acknowledge that there were other features during the GameStop episode ‚Äď such as coordinated behavior of retail investors through social media and their extensive option trading ‚Äď that are missing in our simple specification. Nevertheless, as we show in this section, even this simple specification is sufficient to make our point and generate results consistent with observed patterns. This may be not so surprising since the noise trader demand in our model also could be thought of as the catchall quantity capturing the total stock demand coming from the coordinated behavior of retail investors and option market makers‚Äô hedging demands.

38 For instance, during the GameStop episode, it was reported that a large number of small investors were buying certain ‚Äúmeme‚ÄĚ stocks (as well as buying call options on those stocks) using online trading apps, such as Robinhood. However, these online trading firms do not execute trades directly, but they usually work with large market makers or brokerages, such as Citadel Securities. Thus, according to our model the stabilizing effect could be achieved if these large market makers and brokerages, who ultimately hold the securities, were induced to lend the stock shares in the short-selling market on behalf of small investors. Alternatively, one could also think of the noise traders‚Äô lending fraction in our model as being the call options trading by small investors. This is because option market makers, who are on the other side of these trades, typically delta-hedge their positions by buying stock shares. Since option market makers are typically large firms who are more likely to have access to the stock lending market, a higher call options trading in reality could be mapped to the noise traders‚Äô lending fraction in our model.
